revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Temple and Shrine Authorities forks]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Jisha Seiryoku''' (寺社勢力) refers to the religious and temple powers of Japan, also known as '''Shaji Seiryoku''' (社寺勢力).<ref>[[Hiraizumi Kiyomi]] used the term "Shaji" and "Shaji Seiryoku" in his work *The Relationship Between Temples and Society in the Middle Ages* (1926).</ref> These powers were significant during Japan's {{ill|medieval period|ja|日本の中世}}, sharing authority with the {{ill|Bakufu|ja|武家政権}} and the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷}}. Unlike centralized political entities, the religious powers were decentralized, with each temple or shrine acting independently. Even within a single temple or shrine, various factions often vied for dominance, which is why the term "seiryoku" (power) is used.
== Overview == During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, major temples such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}} and {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} established their own military forces, including {{ill|Sohei|ja|僧兵}} (warrior monks), and utilized demonstrations such as carrying {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} in {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴}} to assert their demands.
Temples also maintained large entourages of lay followers known as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒}} and {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人}}, enabling significant economic, academic, and artistic activities. The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple complexes as urban areas|ja|境内都市}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
{{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}} and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} (modern {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}), dominated eastern Kyoto, while {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).
Within the temple domains, the {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|ja|アジール}} regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}, as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, leaders like {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building {{ill|Azuchi Castle|ja|安土城}} and {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺}} firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.
== Major Religious Powers == * {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}} {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}} * {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}} * {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} (now {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}) * {{ill|Kiyomizu-dera|ja|清水寺}} * {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮}} * {{ill|Shitennō-ji|ja|四天王寺}} * {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺}} * {{ill|Ikkō-shū|ja|一向宗}} ({{ill|Jōdo Shinshū Honganji-ha|ja|浄土真宗本願寺派}}) ** {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji|ja|石山本願寺}} ** {{ill|Yamashina Hongan-ji|ja|山科本願寺}} ** {{ill|Oyama Gobo|ja|尾山御坊}} ** {{ill|Yoshizaki Gobo|ja|吉崎御坊}} ** {{ill|Ikkō Ikki|ja|一向一揆}} * {{ill|Senchū-ji|ja|専修寺}} ({{ill|Shinshū Takada-ha|ja|真宗高田派}}) * {{ill|Kimpusen-ji|ja|金峯山寺}} (Yoshino) * {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} * {{ill|Daigo-ji|ja|醍醐寺}} * {{ill|Kongōbu-ji|ja|金剛峯寺}} ({{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}}) * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Shōgoin|ja|聖護院}} * {{ill|Onjō-ji|ja|園城寺}} * {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺}} * {{ill|Dewa Sanzan|ja|出羽三山}} * {{ill|Heisen-ji|ja|平泉寺}} (now {{ill|Heisen-ji Hakusan Shrine|ja|平泉寺白山神社}}) * {{ill|Isurugi-yama|ja|石動山}} Tenpyō-ji (now {{ill|Isurugi Hikojinja|ja|伊須流岐比古神社}}) * {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰}} Myōraku-ji (now {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|ja|談山神社}}) * {{ill|Ōyama-dera|ja|大山寺}} * {{ill|Suwa-taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} * {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}} * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐神宮}} * {{ill|Aso Shrine|ja|阿蘇神社}} * {{ill|Hiko-san|ja|英彦山}} Reisō-ji
== References == <references />
== Bibliography == * {{ill|Kuroda Toshio|ja|黒田俊雄}}. *Temple and Shrine Powers: Another Medieval Society.* Iwanami Shinsho, 1980. * {{ill|Itō Masatoshi|ja|伊藤正敏}}. *The Medieval Religious Powers: Migration and Connection.* Chikuma Shinsho, 2008.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kenmitsu System|ja|顕密体制|ja|顕密体制}} * {{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制|ja|権門体制}} * {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|ja|アジール}} * {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|ja|境内都市}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|fr|Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|lt=Teramachi|ja|寺内町}} * {{ill|Sōhei|ja|僧兵}} * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人}}

'''Jisha Seiryoku''' (寺社勢力) refers to the religious and temple powers of Japan, also known as '''Shaji Seiryoku''' (社寺勢力).<ref>[[Hiraizumi Kiyomi]] used the term "Shaji" and "Shaji Seiryoku" in his work *The Relationship Between Temples and Society in the Middle Ages* (1926).</ref> These powers were significant during Japan's {{ill|medieval period|ja|日本の中世}}, sharing authority with the {{ill|Bakufu|ja|武家政権}} and the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷}}. Unlike centralized political entities, the religious powers were decentralized, with each temple or shrine acting independently. Even within a single temple or shrine, various factions often vied for dominance, which is why the term "seiryoku" (power) is used.
== Overview == During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, major temples such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}} and {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} established their own military forces, including {{ill|Sohei|ja|僧兵}} (warrior monks), and utilized demonstrations such as carrying {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} in {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴}} to assert their demands.
Temples also maintained large entourages of lay followers known as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒}} and {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人}}, enabling significant economic, academic, and artistic activities. The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple complexes as urban areas|ja|境内都市}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
{{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}} and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} (modern {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}), dominated eastern Kyoto, while {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).
Within the temple domains, the {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|ja|アジール}} regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}, as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, leaders like {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building {{ill|Azuchi Castle|ja|安土城}} and {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺}} firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.
== Major Religious Powers == * {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}} {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}} * {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}} * {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} (now {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}) * {{ill|Kiyomizu-dera|ja|清水寺}} * {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮}} * {{ill|Shitennō-ji|ja|四天王寺}} * {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺}} * {{ill|Ikkō-shū|ja|一向宗}} ({{ill|Jōdo Shinshū Honganji-ha|ja|浄土真宗本願寺派}}) ** {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji|ja|石山本願寺}} ** {{ill|Yamashina Hongan-ji|ja|山科本願寺}} ** {{ill|Oyama Gobo|ja|尾山御坊}} ** {{ill|Yoshizaki Gobo|ja|吉崎御坊}} ** {{ill|Ikkō Ikki|ja|一向一揆}} * {{ill|Senchū-ji|ja|専修寺}} ({{ill|Shinshū Takada-ha|ja|真宗高田派}}) * {{ill|Kimpusen-ji|ja|金峯山寺}} (Yoshino) * {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} * {{ill|Daigo-ji|ja|醍醐寺}} * {{ill|Kongōbu-ji|ja|金剛峯寺}} ({{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}}) * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Shōgoin|ja|聖護院}} * {{ill|Onjō-ji|ja|園城寺}} * {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺}} * {{ill|Dewa Sanzan|ja|出羽三山}} * {{ill|Heisen-ji|ja|平泉寺}} (now {{ill|Heisen-ji Hakusan Shrine|ja|平泉寺白山神社}}) * {{ill|Isurugi-yama|ja|石動山}} Tenpyō-ji (now {{ill|Isurugi Hikojinja|ja|伊須流岐比古神社}}) * {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰}} Myōraku-ji (now {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|ja|談山神社}}) * {{ill|Ōyama-dera|ja|大山寺}} * {{ill|Suwa-taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} * {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}} * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐神宮}} * {{ill|Aso Shrine|ja|阿蘇神社}} * {{ill|Hiko-san|ja|英彦山}} Reisō-ji
== References == <references />
== Bibliography == * {{ill|Kuroda Toshio|ja|黒田俊雄}}. *Temple and Shrine Powers: Another Medieval Society.* Iwanami Shinsho, 1980. * {{ill|Itō Masatoshi|ja|伊藤正敏}}. *The Medieval Religious Powers: Migration and Connection.* Chikuma Shinsho, 2008.

'''Jisha Seiryoku''' (寺社勢力) refers to the religious and temple powers of Japan, also known as '''Shaji Seiryoku''' (社寺勢力).<ref>[[Hiraizumi Kiyomi]] used the term "Shaji" and "Shaji Seiryoku" in his work *The Relationship Between Temples and Society in the Middle Ages* (1926).</ref> These powers were significant during Japan's {{ill|medieval period|ja|日本の中世}}, sharing authority with the {{ill|Bakufu|ja|武家政権}} and the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷}}. Unlike centralized political entities, the religious powers were decentralized, with each temple or shrine acting independently. Even within a single temple or shrine, various factions often vied for dominance, which is why the term "seiryoku" (power) is used.
== Overview == During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, major temples such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}} and {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} established their own military forces, including {{ill|Sohei|ja|僧兵}} (warrior monks), and utilized demonstrations such as carrying {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} in {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴}} to assert their demands.
Temples also maintained large entourages of lay followers known as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒}} and {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人}}, enabling significant economic, academic, and artistic activities. The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple complexes as urban areas|ja|境内都市}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
{{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}} and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} (modern {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}), dominated eastern Kyoto, while {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).
Within the temple domains, the {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|ja|アジール}} regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}, as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, leaders like {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building {{ill|Azuchi Castle|ja|安土城}} and {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺}} firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.
== Major Religious Powers == * {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}} {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}} * {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}} * {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} (now {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}) * {{ill|Kiyomizu-dera|ja|清水寺}} * {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮}} * {{ill|Shitennō-ji|ja|四天王寺}} * {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺}} * {{ill|Ikkō-shū|ja|一向宗}} ({{ill|Jōdo Shinshū Honganji-ha|ja|浄土真宗本願寺派}}) ** {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji|ja|石山本願寺}} ** {{ill|Yamashina Hongan-ji|ja|山科本願寺}} ** {{ill|Oyama Gobo|ja|尾山御坊}} ** {{ill|Yoshizaki Gobo|ja|吉崎御坊}} ** {{ill|Ikkō Ikki|ja|一向一揆}} * {{ill|Senchū-ji|ja|専修寺}} ({{ill|Shinshū Takada-ha|ja|真宗高田派}}) * {{ill|Kimpusen-ji|ja|金峯山寺}} (Yoshino) * {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} * {{ill|Daigo-ji|ja|醍醐寺}} * {{ill|Kongōbu-ji|ja|金剛峯寺}} ({{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}}) * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Shōgoin|ja|聖護院}} * {{ill|Onjō-ji|ja|園城寺}} * {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺}} * {{ill|Dewa Sanzan|ja|出羽三山}} * {{ill|Heisen-ji|ja|平泉寺}} (now {{ill|Heisen-ji Hakusan Shrine|ja|平泉寺白山神社}}) * {{ill|Isurugi-yama|ja|石動山}} Tenpyō-ji (now {{ill|Isurugi Hikojinja|ja|伊須流岐比古神社}}) * {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰}} Myōraku-ji (now {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|ja|談山神社}}) * {{ill|Ōyama-dera|ja|大山寺}} * {{ill|Suwa-taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} * {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}} * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐神宮}} * {{ill|Aso Shrine|ja|阿蘇神社}} * {{ill|Hiko-san|ja|英彦山}} Reisō-ji
== References == <references />
== Bibliography == * {{ill|Kuroda Toshio|ja|黒田俊雄}}. *Temple and Shrine Powers: Another Medieval Society.* Iwanami Shinsho, 1980. * {{ill|Itō Masatoshi|ja|伊藤正敏}}. *The Medieval Religious Powers: Migration and Connection.* Chikuma Shinsho, 2008.== See Also == * {{ill|Kenmitsu System|ja|顕密体制|ja|顕密体制}} * {{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制|ja|権門体制}} * {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|ja|アジール}} * {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|ja|境内都市}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|fr|Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|lt=Teramachi|ja|寺内町}} * {{ill|Sōhei|ja|僧兵}} * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "== See Also! ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
'''Jisha Seiryoku''' (寺社勢力) refers to the religious and temple powers of Japan, also known as '''Shaji Seiryoku''' (社寺勢力).<ref>[[Hiraizumi Kiyomi]] used the term "Shaji" and "Shaji Seiryoku" in his work *The Relationship Between Temples and Society in the Middle Ages* (1926).</ref> These powers were significant during Japan's {{ill|medieval period|ja|日本の中世}}, sharing authority with the {{ill|Bakufu|ja|武家政権}} and the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷}}. Unlike centralized political entities, the religious powers were decentralized, with each temple or shrine acting independently. Even within a single temple or shrine, various factions often vied for dominance, which is why the term "seiryoku" (power) is used.
== Overview == During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, major temples such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}} and {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} established their own military forces, including {{ill|Sohei|ja|僧兵}} (warrior monks), and utilized demonstrations such as carrying {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} in {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴}} to assert their demands.
Temples also maintained large entourages of lay followers known as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒}} and {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人}}, enabling significant economic, academic, and artistic activities. The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple complexes as urban areas|ja|境内都市}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
{{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}} and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} (modern {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}), dominated eastern Kyoto, while {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).
Within the temple domains, the {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|ja|アジール}} regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}, as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, leaders like {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building {{ill|Azuchi Castle|ja|安土城}} and {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺}} firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.
== Major Religious Powers == * {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}} {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}} * {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}} * {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} (now {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}) * {{ill|Kiyomizu-dera|ja|清水寺}} * {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮}} * {{ill|Shitennō-ji|ja|四天王寺}} * {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺}} * {{ill|Ikkō-shū|ja|一向宗}} ({{ill|Jōdo Shinshū Honganji-ha|ja|浄土真宗本願寺派}}) ** {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji|ja|石山本願寺}} ** {{ill|Yamashina Hongan-ji|ja|山科本願寺}} ** {{ill|Oyama Gobo|ja|尾山御坊}} ** {{ill|Yoshizaki Gobo|ja|吉崎御坊}} ** {{ill|Ikkō Ikki|ja|一向一揆}} * {{ill|Senchū-ji|ja|専修寺}} ({{ill|Shinshū Takada-ha|ja|真宗高田派}}) * {{ill|Kimpusen-ji|ja|金峯山寺}} (Yoshino) * {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} * {{ill|Daigo-ji|ja|醍醐寺}} * {{ill|Kongōbu-ji|ja|金剛峯寺}} ({{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}}) * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Shōgoin|ja|聖護院}} * {{ill|Onjō-ji|ja|園城寺}} * {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺}} * {{ill|Dewa Sanzan|ja|出羽三山}} * {{ill|Heisen-ji|ja|平泉寺}} (now {{ill|Heisen-ji Hakusan Shrine|ja|平泉寺白山神社}}) * {{ill|Isurugi-yama|ja|石動山}} Tenpyō-ji (now {{ill|Isurugi Hikojinja|ja|伊須流岐比古神社}}) * {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰}} Myōraku-ji (now {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|ja|談山神社}}) * {{ill|Ōyama-dera|ja|大山寺}} * {{ill|Suwa-taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} * {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}} * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐神宮}} * {{ill|Aso Shrine|ja|阿蘇神社}} * {{ill|Hiko-san|ja|英彦山}} Reisō-ji
== References == <references />
== Bibliography == * {{ill|Kuroda Toshio|ja|黒田俊雄}}. *Temple and Shrine Powers: Another Medieval Society.* Iwanami Shinsho, 1980. * {{ill|Itō Masatoshi|ja|伊藤正敏}}. *The Medieval Religious Powers: Migration and Connection.* Chikuma Shinsho, 2008. == See Also == * {{ill|Kenmitsu System|ja|顕密体制|ja|顕密体制}} * {{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制|ja|権門体制}} * {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|ja|アジール}} * {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|ja|境内都市}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|fr|Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|lt=Teramachi|ja|寺内町}} * {{ill|Sōhei|ja|僧兵}} * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人}}

Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building {{ill|Azuchi Castle|ja|安土城}} and {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺}} firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.
== Major Religious Powers == * {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}} {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}} * {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}} * {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} (now {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}) * {{ill|Kiyomizu-dera|ja|清水寺}} * {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮}} * {{ill|Shitennō-ji|ja|四天王寺}} * {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺}} * {{ill|Ikkō-shū|ja|一向宗}} ({{ill|Jōdo Shinshū Honganji-ha|ja|浄土真宗本願寺派}}) ** {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji|ja|石山本願寺}} ** {{ill|Yamashina Hongan-ji|ja|山科本願寺}} ** {{ill|Oyama Gobo|ja|尾山御坊}} ** {{ill|Yoshizaki Gobo|ja|吉崎御坊}} ** {{ill|Ikkō Ikki|ja|一向一揆}} * {{ill|Senchū-ji|ja|専修寺}} ({{ill|Shinshū Takada-ha|ja|真宗高田派}}) * {{ill|Kimpusen-ji|ja|金峯山寺}} (Yoshino) * {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} * {{ill|Daigo-ji|ja|醍醐寺}} * {{ill|Kongōbu-ji|ja|金剛峯寺}} ({{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}}) * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Shōgoin|ja|聖護院}} * {{ill|Onjō-ji|ja|園城寺}} * {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺}} * {{ill|Dewa Sanzan|ja|出羽三山}} * {{ill|Heisen-ji|ja|平泉寺}} (now {{ill|Heisen-ji Hakusan Shrine|ja|平泉寺白山神社}}) * {{ill|Isurugi-yama|ja|石動山}} Tenpyō-ji (now {{ill|Isurugi Hikojinja|ja|伊須流岐比古神社}}) * {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰}} Myōraku-ji (now {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|ja|談山神社}}) * {{ill|Ōyama-dera|ja|大山寺}} * {{ill|Suwa-taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} * {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}} * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐神宮}} * {{ill|Aso Shrine|ja|阿蘇神社}} * {{ill|Hiko-san|ja|英彦山}} Reisō-ji

'''Jisha Seiryoku''' (寺社勢力) refers to the religious and temple powers of Japan, also known as '''Shaji Seiryoku''' (社寺勢力).<ref>[[Hiraizumi Kiyomi]] used the term "Shaji" and "Shaji Seiryoku" in his work *The Relationship Between Temples and Society in the Middle Ages* (1926).</ref> These powers were significant during Japan's {{ill|medieval period|ja|日本の中世}}, sharing authority with the {{ill|Bakufu|ja|武家政権}} and the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷}}. Unlike centralized political entities, the religious powers were decentralized, with each temple or shrine acting independently. Even within a single temple or shrine, various factions often vied for dominance, which is why the term "seiryoku" (power) is used.
== Overview == During the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, major temples such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}} and {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} established their own military forces, including {{ill|Sohei|ja|僧兵}} (warrior monks), and utilized demonstrations such as carrying {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} in {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴}} to assert their demands.
Temples also maintained large entourages of lay followers known as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒}} and {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人}}, enabling significant economic, academic, and artistic activities. The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple complexes as urban areas|ja|境内都市}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
{{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}} and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} (modern {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}), dominated eastern Kyoto, while {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).
Within the temple domains, the {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|ja|アジール}} regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}, as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, leaders like {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building {{ill|Azuchi Castle|ja|安土城}} and {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺}} firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.
== Major Religious Powers == * {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}} {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}} * {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}} * {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} (now {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}) * {{ill|Kiyomizu-dera|ja|清水寺}} * {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮}} * {{ill|Shitennō-ji|ja|四天王寺}} * {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺}} * {{ill|Ikkō-shū|ja|一向宗}} ({{ill|Jōdo Shinshū Honganji-ha|ja|浄土真宗本願寺派}}) ** {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji|ja|石山本願寺}} ** {{ill|Yamashina Hongan-ji|ja|山科本願寺}} ** {{ill|Oyama Gobo|ja|尾山御坊}} ** {{ill|Yoshizaki Gobo|ja|吉崎御坊}} ** {{ill|Ikkō Ikki|ja|一向一揆}} * {{ill|Senchū-ji|ja|専修寺}} ({{ill|Shinshū Takada-ha|ja|真宗高田派}}) * {{ill|Kimpusen-ji|ja|金峯山寺}} (Yoshino) * {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} * {{ill|Daigo-ji|ja|醍醐寺}} * {{ill|Kongōbu-ji|ja|金剛峯寺}} ({{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}}) * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Shōgoin|ja|聖護院}} * {{ill|Onjō-ji|ja|園城寺}} * {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺}} * {{ill|Dewa Sanzan|ja|出羽三山}} * {{ill|Heisen-ji|ja|平泉寺}} (now {{ill|Heisen-ji Hakusan Shrine|ja|平泉寺白山神社}}) * {{ill|Isurugi-yama|ja|石動山}} Tenpyō-ji (now {{ill|Isurugi Hikojinja|ja|伊須流岐比古神社}}) * {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰}} Myōraku-ji (now {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|ja|談山神社}}) * {{ill|Ōyama-dera|ja|大山寺}} * {{ill|Suwa-taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} * {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|ja|厳島神社}} * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|ja|宗像大社}} * {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐神宮}} * {{ill|Aso Shrine|ja|阿蘇神社}} * {{ill|Hiko-san|ja|英彦山}} Reisō-ji

revision.text:
'''寺社勢力'''（じしゃせいりょく）とは、'''社寺勢力'''（しゃじせいりょく）<ref>[[平泉澄]]は著書『中世に於ける社寺と社会の関係』（1926年）にて、「社寺」・「社寺勢力」の用語を用いている。</ref>とも呼ばれ、日本[[中世]]において、[[武家政権]]・[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]とともに権力を三分した、大寺院・神社（当時は[[神仏習合]]のために一体）による[[軍事]]・[[行政]]・[[経済]]・[[文化]]パワーである。武家政権や朝廷のように権力中枢があったわけではなく、各寺社が独立して行動し、また一寺社内でもさまざまな集団がせめぎ合っていたため、「勢力」と呼ぶ。
== 概要 == [[平安時代]]末期、[[延暦寺]]、[[興福寺]]大寺社[[僧兵]]抱えて独自の武力を備え、また[[神輿]]を担いで[[強訴]]を行い、自身の要求を主張するようになった。
また、衆徒・[[神人]]とよばれる俗人を多数配下において大人口を誇り、経済・学問・工芸活動などが盛んだった寺社周辺は、近年の日本歴史学で「[[境内都市]]」と呼ばれる一大[[メガロポリス]]であった。
延暦寺は、配下においていた[[八坂神社|祇園社]]が京の鴨川の東側に大きな境内（領地）を持っていたこと、興福寺は大和国一国の荘園のほとんどを領して中世を通してその経済力で京に大きな支配力を及ぼした。強大な寺社勢力である延暦寺と興福寺を合わせて「'''南都北嶺'''」（なんとほくれい）と称された。
また、大寺社内は「[[アジール|無縁所]]」とよばれる地域であり、生活に困窮した庶民が多く移民し、寺社領地内に吸収された。また、幕府が罪人を捜査する「[[不入の権 (日本)#検断不入|検断権]]」も大寺社内には及ばず、そのため[[源義経]]や[[後醍醐天皇]]など、戦乱に追われた人々の多くが寺社にかくまわれた。
[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]末期において、[[織田信長]]、[[豊臣秀吉]]などは寺社勢力と激しく敵対し、苛烈な戦いを繰り広げた。だがその一方で、[[安土城]]の築城に延暦寺の職人を利用したり、[[根来寺]]の鉄砲を戦争に利用するなど、寺社勢力の高い技術力を活用もした。
豊臣秀吉によって1585年<!--豊臣秀吉の刀狩令は全国的に出された1588年の刀狩令が有名であるが、1585年にも一部寺社勢力に対して刀狩がだされているため、「1585年から」と記述する。-->から1588年にかけて出された[[刀狩|刀狩令]]は百姓等のみならず寺社勢力の武器没収も意味しており、この結果として約五百年間続いた寺社勢力は日本の権力構造から消えることとなった。
== 主な寺社勢力 == *[[比叡山]] [[延暦寺]] *[[日吉大社]] *[[祇園神社|祇園社]]（現：[[八坂神社]]） *[[清水寺]] *石清水八幡宮寺（現：[[石清水八幡宮]]） *[[四天王寺]] *[[東大寺]] *[[一向宗]]([[浄土真宗本願寺派]]) **[[石山本願寺]] **[[山科本願寺]] **[[尾山御坊]] **[[吉崎御坊]] **[[一向一揆]] *[[専修寺]]([[真宗高田派]]) *[[金峯山寺]]（吉野） *[[興福寺]] *[[醍醐寺]] *[[高野山]] [[金剛峯寺]] *[[熊野三山]] *[[聖護院]] *[[園城寺]] *[[根来寺]] *[[出羽三山]] *平泉寺（現：[[平泉寺白山神社]]） *[[石動山]] 天平寺（現：[[伊須流岐比古神社]]） *[[多武峰]] 妙楽寺（現：[[談山神社]]） *[[大山寺]] *[[諏訪大社]] *[[厳島神社]] *[[宗像大社]] *[[宇佐神宮]] *[[阿蘇神社]] *[[英彦山神宮|英彦山 霊仙寺]]
== 脚注 == <references />
== 参考文献 == *[[黒田俊雄]]『寺社勢力 もう一つの中世社会』岩波新書 1980年 *[[伊藤正敏]]『寺社勢力の中世 無縁・有縁・移民』ちくま新書 2008年
== 関連項目 == *[[顕密体制]] *[[権門体制]] *[[アジール]] *[[境内都市]] *[[寺内町]] *[[僧兵]] *[[神人]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[寺社勢力]] to [[Temple and Shrine Powers]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
'''寺社勢力'''（じしゃせいりょく）とは、'''社寺勢力'''（しゃじせいりょく）<ref>[[平泉澄]]は著書『中世に於ける社寺と社会の関係』（1926年）にて、「社寺」・「社寺勢力」の用語を用いている。</ref>とも呼ばれ、日本[[中世]]において、[[武家政権]]・[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]とともに権力を三分した、大寺院・神社（当時は[[神仏習合]]のために一体）による[[軍事]]・[[行政]]・[[経済]]・[[文化]]パワーである。武家政権や朝廷のように権力中枢があったわけではなく、各寺社が独立して行動し、また一寺社内でもさまざまな集団がせめぎ合っていたため、「勢力」と呼ぶ。
== 概要 == [[平安時代]]末期、[[延暦寺]]、[[興福寺]]大寺社[[僧兵]]抱えて独自の武力を備え、また[[神輿]]を担いで[[強訴]]を行い、自身の要求を主張するようになった。
また、衆徒・[[神人]]とよばれる俗人を多数配下において大人口を誇り、経済・学問・工芸活動などが盛んだった寺社周辺は、近年の日本歴史学で「[[境内都市]]」と呼ばれる一大[[メガロポリス]]であった。
延暦寺は、配下においていた[[八坂神社|祇園社]]が京の鴨川の東側に大きな境内（領地）を持っていたこと、興福寺は大和国一国の荘園のほとんどを領して中世を通してその経済力で京に大きな支配力を及ぼした。強大な寺社勢力である延暦寺と興福寺を合わせて「'''南都北嶺'''」（なんとほくれい）と称された。
また、大寺社内は「[[アジール|無縁所]]」とよばれる地域であり、生活に困窮した庶民が多く移民し、寺社領地内に吸収された。また、幕府が罪人を捜査する「[[不入の権 (日本)#検断不入|検断権]]」も大寺社内には及ばず、そのため[[源義経]]や[[後醍醐天皇]]など、戦乱に追われた人々の多くが寺社にかくまわれた。
[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]末期において、[[織田信長]]、[[豊臣秀吉]]などは寺社勢力と激しく敵対し、苛烈な戦いを繰り広げた。だがその一方で、[[安土城]]の築城に延暦寺の職人を利用したり、[[根来寺]]の鉄砲を戦争に利用するなど、寺社勢力の高い技術力を活用もした。
豊臣秀吉によって1585年<!--豊臣秀吉の刀狩令は全国的に出された1588年の刀狩令が有名であるが、1585年にも一部寺社勢力に対して刀狩がだされているため、「1585年から」と記述する。-->から1588年にかけて出された[[刀狩|刀狩令]]は百姓等のみならず寺社勢力の武器没収も意味しており、この結果として約五百年間続いた寺社勢力は日本の権力構造から消えることとなった。
== 主な寺社勢力 == *[[比叡山]] [[延暦寺]] *[[日吉大社]] *[[祇園神社|祇園社]]（現：[[八坂神社]]） *[[清水寺]] *石清水八幡宮寺（現：[[石清水八幡宮]]） *[[四天王寺]] *[[東大寺]] *[[一向宗]]([[浄土真宗本願寺派]]) **[[石山本願寺]] **[[山科本願寺]] **[[尾山御坊]] **[[吉崎御坊]] **[[一向一揆]] *[[専修寺]]([[真宗高田派]]) *[[金峯山寺]]（吉野） *[[興福寺]] *[[醍醐寺]] *[[高野山]] [[金剛峯寺]] *[[熊野三山]] *[[聖護院]] *[[園城寺]] *[[根来寺]] *[[出羽三山]] *平泉寺（現：[[平泉寺白山神社]]） *[[石動山]] 天平寺（現：[[伊須流岐比古神社]]） *[[多武峰]] 妙楽寺（現：[[談山神社]]） *[[大山寺]] *[[諏訪大社]] *[[厳島神社]] *[[宗像大社]] *[[宇佐神宮]] *[[阿蘇神社]] *[[英彦山神宮|英彦山 霊仙寺]]
== 脚注 == <references />
== 参考文献 == *[[黒田俊雄]]『寺社勢力 もう一つの中世社会』岩波新書 1980年 *[[伊藤正敏]]『寺社勢力の中世 無縁・有縁・移民』ちくま新書 2008年
== 関連項目 == *[[顕密体制]] *[[権門体制]] *[[アジール]] *[[境内都市]] *[[寺内町]] *[[僧兵]] *[[神人]]

revision.text:
'''Jisha Seiryoku''' (寺社勢力) refers to the religious and temple powers of Japan, also known as '''Shaji Seiryoku''' (社寺勢力).<ref>[[Hiraizumi Kiyomi]] used the term "Shaji" and "Shaji Seiryoku" in his work *The Relationship Between Temples and Society in the Middle Ages* (1926).</ref> These powers were significant during Japan's {{ill|medieval period|ja|日本の中世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period|qq=}}, sharing authority with the {{ill|Bakufu|ja|武家政権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|qq=}} and the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court of Japan]]. Unlike centralized political entities, the religious powers were decentralized, with each temple or shrine acting independently. Even within a single temple or shrine, various factions often vied for dominance, which is why the term "seiryoku" (power) is used.
== Overview == During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], major temples such as [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] established their own military forces, including [[:en:Sōhei|Sohei]] (warrior monks), and utilized demonstrations such as carrying [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] in {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goso|qq=}} to assert their demands.
Temples also maintained large entourages of lay followers known as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}, enabling significant economic, academic, and artistic activities. The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
[[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|qq=}} (modern [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]), dominated eastern Kyoto, while [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).
Within the temple domains, the {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Right of asylum|qq=}} regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan-funyu|qq=}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, leaders like [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building [[:en:Azuchi Castle|Azuchi Castle]] and [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|qq=}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.
== Major Religious Powers == * [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] * [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|qq=}} (now [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]) * [[:en:Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu-dera]] * [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[:en:Shitennō-ji|Shitennō-ji]] * [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] * [[:en:Ikkō-shū|Ikkō-shū]] ([[:en:Jodo Shinshu Hongwanji-ha|Jōdo Shinshū Honganji-ha]]) ** [[:en:Ishiyama Hongan-ji|Ishiyama Hongan-ji]] ** [[:en:Yamashina Mido|Yamashina Hongan-ji]] ** {{ill|Oyama Gobo|ja|尾山御坊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Gobo|qq=}} ** [[:en:Yoshizaki-gobō|Yoshizaki Gobo]] ** [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō Ikki]] * [[:en:Senju-ji|Senchū-ji]] ({{ill|Shinshū Takada-ha|ja|真宗高田派|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshū Takada-ha|qq=}}) * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kimpusen-ji]] (Yoshino) * [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] * [[:en:Daigo-ji|Daigo-ji]] * [[:en:Kongōbu-ji|Kongōbu-ji]] ([[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]) * {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} * [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] * [[:en:Mii-dera|Onjō-ji]] * [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] * [[:en:Three Mountains of Dewa|Dewa Sanzan]] * {{ill|Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisen-ji|ja|平泉寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisen-ji|qq=}} (now [[:en:Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|Heisen-ji Hakusan Shrine]]) * [[:en:Mount Sekidō|Isurugi-yama]] Tenpyō-ji (now {{ill|Isurugi Hikojinja|ja|伊須流岐比古神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isurugi Hikojinja|qq=}}) * {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnomine|qq=}} Myōraku-ji (now [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]]) * {{ill|Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyama-dera|ja|大山寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyama-dera|qq=}} * [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] * [[:en:Itsukushima Shrine|Itsukushima Shrine]] * [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingū]] * [[:en:Aso Shrine|Aso Shrine]] * [[:en:Mount Hiko|Hiko-san]] Reisō-ji
== References == <references />
== Bibliography == * [[:en:Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|Kuroda Toshio]]. *Temple and Shrine Powers: Another Medieval Society.* Iwanami Shinsho, 1980. * {{ill|Itō Masatoshi|ja|伊藤正敏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Masatoshi|qq=}}. *The Medieval Religious Powers: Migration and Connection.* Chikuma Shinsho, 2008. == See Also == * {{ill|Kenmitsu System|ja|顕密体制|ja|顕密体制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmitsu System|qq=}} * {{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制|ja|権門体制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon System Theory|qq=}} * {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Right of asylum|qq=}} * {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|ja|境内都市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple Precinct Cities|qq=}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|fr|Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|lt=Teramachi|ja|寺内町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaimachi|qq=}} * [[:en:Sōhei|Sōhei]] * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:History of Buddhism in Japan
revision.text:
'''Jisha Seiryoku''' (寺社勢力) refers to the religious and temple powers of Japan, also known as '''Shaji Seiryoku''' (社寺勢力).<ref>[[Hiraizumi Kiyomi]] used the term "Shaji" and "Shaji Seiryoku" in his work *The Relationship Between Temples and Society in the Middle Ages* (1926).</ref> These powers were significant during Japan's {{ill|medieval period|ja|日本の中世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period|qq=}}, sharing authority with the {{ill|Bakufu|ja|武家政権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|qq=}} and the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court of Japan]]. Unlike centralized political entities, the religious powers were decentralized, with each temple or shrine acting independently. Even within a single temple or shrine, various factions often vied for dominance, which is why the term "seiryoku" (power) is used.
== Overview == During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], major temples such as [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] established their own military forces, including [[:en:Sōhei|Sohei]] (warrior monks), and utilized demonstrations such as carrying [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] in {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goso|qq=}} to assert their demands.
Temples also maintained large entourages of lay followers known as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}, enabling significant economic, academic, and artistic activities. The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
[[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|qq=}} (modern [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]), dominated eastern Kyoto, while [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).
Within the temple domains, the {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Right of asylum|qq=}} regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan-funyu|qq=}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, leaders like [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building [[:en:Azuchi Castle|Azuchi Castle]] and [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|qq=}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.
== Major Religious Powers == * [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] * [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|qq=}} (now [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]) * [[:en:Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu-dera]] * [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[:en:Shitennō-ji|Shitennō-ji]] * [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] * [[:en:Ikkō-shū|Ikkō-shū]] ([[:en:Jodo Shinshu Hongwanji-ha|Jōdo Shinshū Honganji-ha]]) ** [[:en:Ishiyama Hongan-ji|Ishiyama Hongan-ji]] ** [[:en:Yamashina Mido|Yamashina Hongan-ji]] ** {{ill|Oyama Gobo|ja|尾山御坊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Gobo|qq=}} ** [[:en:Yoshizaki-gobō|Yoshizaki Gobo]] ** [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō Ikki]] * [[:en:Senju-ji|Senchū-ji]] ({{ill|Shinshū Takada-ha|ja|真宗高田派|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshū Takada-ha|qq=}}) * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kimpusen-ji]] (Yoshino) * [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] * [[:en:Daigo-ji|Daigo-ji]] * [[:en:Kongōbu-ji|Kongōbu-ji]] ([[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]) * {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} * [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] * [[:en:Mii-dera|Onjō-ji]] * [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] * [[:en:Three Mountains of Dewa|Dewa Sanzan]] * {{ill|Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisen-ji|ja|平泉寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisen-ji|qq=}} (now [[:en:Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|Heisen-ji Hakusan Shrine]]) * [[:en:Mount Sekidō|Isurugi-yama]] Tenpyō-ji (now {{ill|Isurugi Hikojinja|ja|伊須流岐比古神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isurugi Hikojinja|qq=}}) * {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnomine|qq=}} Myōraku-ji (now [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]]) * {{ill|Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyama-dera|ja|大山寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyama-dera|qq=}} * [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] * [[:en:Itsukushima Shrine|Itsukushima Shrine]] * [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingū]] * [[:en:Aso Shrine|Aso Shrine]] * [[:en:Mount Hiko|Hiko-san]] Reisō-ji
== References == <references />
== Bibliography == * [[:en:Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|Kuroda Toshio]]. *Temple and Shrine Powers: Another Medieval Society.* Iwanami Shinsho, 1980. * {{ill|Itō Masatoshi|ja|伊藤正敏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Masatoshi|qq=}}. *The Medieval Religious Powers: Migration and Connection.* Chikuma Shinsho, 2008. == See Also == * {{ill|Kenmitsu System|ja|顕密体制|ja|顕密体制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmitsu System|qq=}} * {{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制|ja|権門体制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon System Theory|qq=}} * {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Right of asylum|qq=}} * {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|ja|境内都市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple Precinct Cities|qq=}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|fr|Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|lt=Teramachi|ja|寺内町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaimachi|qq=}} * [[:en:Sōhei|Sōhei]] * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}

[[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|qq=}} (modern [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]), dominated eastern Kyoto, while [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).
Within the temple domains, the {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Right of asylum|qq=}} regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan-funyu|qq=}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, leaders like [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building [[:en:Azuchi Castle|Azuchi Castle]] and [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|qq=}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.

<!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--> [[Category:Azuchi–Momoyama period]] [[Category:History of Buddhism in Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->

'''Jisha Seiryoku''' (寺社勢力) refers to the religious and temple powers of Japan, also known as '''Shaji Seiryoku''' (社寺勢力).<ref>[[Hiraizumi Kiyomi]] used the term "Shaji" and "Shaji Seiryoku" in his work *The Relationship Between Temples and Society in the Middle Ages* (1926).</ref> These powers were significant during Japan's {{ill|medieval period|ja|日本の中世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, sharing authority with the {{ill|Bakufu|ja|武家政権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|qq=}} and the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court of Japan]]. Unlike centralized political entities, the religious powers were decentralized, with each temple or shrine acting independently. Even within a single temple or shrine, various factions often vied for dominance, which is why the term "seiryoku" (power) is used.
== Overview == During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], major temples such as [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] established their own military forces, including [[:en:Sōhei|Sohei]] (warrior monks), and utilized demonstrations such as carrying [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] in {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goso|qq=}} to assert their demands.
Temples also maintained large entourages of lay followers known as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}, enabling significant economic, academic, and artistic activities. The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
[[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (modern [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]), dominated eastern Kyoto, while [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).
Within the temple domains, the [[:en:Right of asylum|Right of asylum]] regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan-funyu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, leaders like [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building [[:en:Azuchi Castle|Azuchi Castle]] and [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 刀狩}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.

<!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--> [[Category:Azuchi–Momoyama period]] [[Category:History of Buddhism in Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11458053]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Jisha Seiryoku''' (寺社勢力) refers to the religious and temple powers of Japan, also known as '''Shaji Seiryoku''' (社寺勢力).<ref>[[Hiraizumi Kiyomi]] used the term "Shaji" and "Shaji Seiryoku" in his work *The Relationship Between Temples and Society in the Middle Ages* (1926).</ref> These powers were significant during Japan's {{ill|medieval period|ja|日本の中世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, sharing authority with the {{ill|Bakufu|ja|武家政権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|qq=}} and the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court of Japan]]. Unlike centralized political entities, the religious powers were decentralized, with each temple or shrine acting independently. Even within a single temple or shrine, various factions often vied for dominance, which is why the term "seiryoku" (power) is used.
== Overview == During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], major temples such as [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] established their own military forces, including [[:en:Sōhei|Sohei]] (warrior monks), and utilized demonstrations such as carrying [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] in {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goso|qq=}} to assert their demands.
Temples also maintained large entourages of lay followers known as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}, enabling significant economic, academic, and artistic activities. The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
[[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (modern [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]), dominated eastern Kyoto, while [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).
Within the temple domains, the [[:en:Right of asylum|Right of asylum]] regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan-funyu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, leaders like [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building [[:en:Azuchi Castle|Azuchi Castle]] and [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 刀狩}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.
== Major Religious Powers == * [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] * [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (now [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]) * [[:en:Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu-dera]] * [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[:en:Shitennō-ji|Shitennō-ji]] * [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] * [[:en:Ikkō-shū|Ikkō-shū]] ([[:en:Jodo Shinshu Hongwanji-ha|Jōdo Shinshū Honganji-ha]]) ** [[:en:Ishiyama Hongan-ji|Ishiyama Hongan-ji]] ** [[:en:Yamashina Mido|Yamashina Hongan-ji]] ** {{ill|Oyama Gobo|ja|尾山御坊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Gobo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金沢城}} ** [[:en:Yoshizaki-gobō|Yoshizaki Gobo]] ** [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō Ikki]] * [[:en:Senju-ji|Senchū-ji]] ({{ill|Shinshū Takada-ha|ja|真宗高田派|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshū Takada-ha|qq=}}) * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kimpusen-ji]] (Yoshino) * [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] * [[:en:Daigo-ji|Daigo-ji]] * [[:en:Kongōbu-ji|Kongōbu-ji]] ([[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]) * {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} * [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] * [[:en:Mii-dera|Onjō-ji]] * [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] * [[:en:Three Mountains of Dewa|Dewa Sanzan]] * {{ill|Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisen-ji|ja|平泉寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisen-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平泉寺白山神社}} (now [[:en:Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|Heisen-ji Hakusan Shrine]]) * [[:en:Mount Sekidō|Isurugi-yama]] Tenpyō-ji (now {{ill|Isurugi Hikojinja|ja|伊須流岐比古神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isurugi Hikojinja|qq=}}) * {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnomine|qq=}} Myōraku-ji (now [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]]) * {{ill|Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyama-dera|ja|大山寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyama-dera|qq=}} * [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] * [[:en:Itsukushima Shrine|Itsukushima Shrine]] * [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingū]] * [[:en:Aso Shrine|Aso Shrine]] * [[:en:Mount Hiko|Hiko-san]] Reisō-ji
== References == <references />
== Bibliography == * [[:en:Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|Kuroda Toshio]]. *Temple and Shrine Powers: Another Medieval Society.* Iwanami Shinsho, 1980. * {{ill|Itō Masatoshi|ja|伊藤正敏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Masatoshi|qq=}}. *The Medieval Religious Powers: Migration and Connection.* Chikuma Shinsho, 2008. == See Also == * {{ill|Kenmitsu System|ja|顕密体制|ja|顕密体制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmitsu System|qq=}} * {{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制|ja|権門体制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon System Theory|qq=}} * [[:en:Right of asylum|Right of asylum]] * {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|ja|境内都市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple Precinct Cities|qq=}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|fr|Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|lt=Teramachi|ja|寺内町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaimachi|qq=}} * [[:en:Sōhei|Sōhei]] * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}

Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 刀狩}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.
== Major Religious Powers == * [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] * [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (now [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]) * [[sn:Temple and Shrine Powers]] [[ja:寺社勢力]]
<!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--> [[Category:Azuchi–Momoyama period]] [[Category:History of Buddhism in Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Temple and Shrine Powers]][[Category:qq]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11458053]] Wikidata name: [[Category:Add wikidata]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Jisha Seiryoku''' (寺社勢力) refers to the religious and temple powers of Japan, also known as '''Shaji Seiryoku''' (社寺勢力).<ref>[[Hiraizumi Kiyomi]] used the term "Shaji" and "Shaji Seiryoku" in his work *The Relationship Between Temples and Society in the Middle Ages* (1926).</ref> These powers were significant during Japan's {{ill|medieval period|ja|日本の中世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, sharing authority with the {{ill|Bakufu|ja|武家政権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|qq=|1=samurai government|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545907}} and the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court of Japan]]. Unlike centralized political entities, the religious powers were decentralized, with each temple or shrine acting independently. Even within a single temple or shrine, various factions often vied for dominance, which is why the term "seiryoku" (power) is used.
== Overview == During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], major temples such as [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] established their own military forces, including [[:en:Sōhei|Sohei]] (warrior monks), and utilized demonstrations such as carrying [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] in {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goso|qq=}} to assert their demands.
Temples also maintained large entourages of lay followers known as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}, enabling significant economic, academic, and artistic activities. The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
[[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (modern [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]), dominated eastern Kyoto, while [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).
Within the temple domains, the [[:en:Right of asylum|Right of asylum]] regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan-funyu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, leaders like [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building [[:en:Azuchi Castle|Azuchi Castle]] and [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 刀狩|1=Sword hunt|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1133763}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.
== Major Religious Powers == * [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] * [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (now [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]) * [[:en:Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu-dera]] * [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[:en:Shitennō-ji|Shitennō-ji]] * [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] * [[:en:Ikkō-shū|Ikkō-shū]] ([[:en:Jodo Shinshu Hongwanji-ha|Jōdo Shinshū Honganji-ha]]) ** [[:en:Ishiyama Hongan-ji|Ishiyama Hongan-ji]] ** [[:en:Yamashina Mido|Yamashina Hongan-ji]] ** {{ill|Oyama Gobo|ja|尾山御坊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Gobo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金沢城|1=Kanazawa Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q511412}} ** [[:en:Yoshizaki-gobō|Yoshizaki Gobo]] ** [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō Ikki]] * [[:en:Senju-ji|Senchū-ji]] ({{ill|Shinshū Takada-ha|ja|真宗高田派|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshū Takada-ha|qq=}}) * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kimpusen-ji]] (Yoshino) * [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] * [[:en:Daigo-ji|Daigo-ji]] * [[:en:Kongōbu-ji|Kongōbu-ji]] ([[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]) * {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} * [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] * [[:en:Mii-dera|Onjō-ji]] * [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] * [[:en:Three Mountains of Dewa|Dewa Sanzan]] * {{ill|Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisen-ji|ja|平泉寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisen-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平泉寺白山神社}} (now [[:en:Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|Heisen-ji Hakusan Shrine]]) * [[:en:Mount Sekidō|Isurugi-yama]] Tenpyō-ji (now {{ill|Isurugi Hikojinja|ja|伊須流岐比古神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isurugi Hikojinja|qq=}}) * {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnomine|qq=|1=Mount Tōnomine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430808}} Myōraku-ji (now [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]]) * {{ill|Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyama-dera|ja|大山寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyama-dera|qq=}} * [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] * [[:en:Itsukushima Shrine|Itsukushima Shrine]] * [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingū]] * [[:en:Aso Shrine|Aso Shrine]] * [[:en:Mount Hiko|Hiko-san]] Reisō-ji

[[sn:Temple and Shrine Powers]] [[ja:寺社勢力]]
<!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--> [[Category:Azuchi–Momoyama period]] [[Category:History of Buddhism in Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Temple and Shrine Powers]][[Category:qq]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11458053]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Jisha Seiryoku''' (寺社勢力) refers to the religious and temple powers of Japan, also known as '''Shaji Seiryoku''' (社寺勢力).<ref>[[Hiraizumi Kiyomi]] used the term "Shaji" and "Shaji Seiryoku" in his work *The Relationship Between Temples and Society in the Middle Ages* (1926).</ref> These powers were significant during Japan's {{ill|medieval period|ja|日本の中世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, sharing authority with the {{ill|Bakufu|ja|武家政権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|qq=|1=samurai government|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545907}} and the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court of Japan]]. Unlike centralized political entities, the religious powers were decentralized, with each temple or shrine acting independently. Even within a single temple or shrine, various factions often vied for dominance, which is why the term "seiryoku" (power) is used.
== Overview == During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], major temples such as [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] established their own military forces, including [[:en:Sōhei|Sohei]] (warrior monks), and utilized demonstrations such as carrying [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] in {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goso|qq=}} to assert their demands.
Temples also maintained large entourages of lay followers known as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}, enabling significant economic, academic, and artistic activities. The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
[[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (modern [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]), dominated eastern Kyoto, while [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).
Within the temple domains, the [[:en:Right of asylum|Right of asylum]] regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan-funyu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, leaders like [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building [[:en:Azuchi Castle|Azuchi Castle]] and [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 刀狩|1=Sword hunt|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1133763}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.
== Major Religious Powers == * [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] * [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (now [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]) * [[:en:Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu-dera]] * [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[:en:Shitennō-ji|Shitennō-ji]] * [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] * [[:en:Ikkō-shū|Ikkō-shū]] ([[:en:Jodo Shinshu Hongwanji-ha|Jōdo Shinshū Honganji-ha]]) ** [[:en:Ishiyama Hongan-ji|Ishiyama Hongan-ji]] ** [[:en:Yamashina Mido|Yamashina Hongan-ji]] ** {{ill|Oyama Gobo|ja|尾山御坊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Gobo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金沢城|1=Kanazawa Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q511412}} ** [[:en:Mii-dera|Onjō-ji]] * [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] * [[:en:Three Mountains of Dewa|Dewa Sanzan]] * {{ill|Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisen-ji|ja|平泉寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisen-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平泉寺白山神社}} (now [[:en:Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|Heisen-ji Hakusan Shrine]]) * {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnomine|qq=|1=Mount Tōnomine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430808}} Myōraku-ji (now [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]]) * {{ill|Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyama-dera|ja|大山寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyama-dera|qq=}} * [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] * [[:en:Itsukushima Shrine|Itsukushima Shrine]] * [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingū]] * [[:en:Aso Shrine|Aso Shrine]] * [[:en:Mount Hiko|Hiko-san]] Reisō-ji

[[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (modern [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]), dominated eastern Kyoto, while [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).
Within the temple domains, the [[:en:Right of asylum|Right of asylum]] regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan-funyu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, leaders like [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building [[:en:Azuchi Castle|Azuchi Castle]] and [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 刀狩|1=Sword hunt|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1133763}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.

<!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--> [[Category:Azuchi–Momoyama period]] [[Category:History of Buddhism in Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11458053}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社勢力]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11458053]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Jisha Seiryoku''' (寺社勢力) refers to the religious and temple powers of Japan, also known as '''Shaji Seiryoku''' (社寺勢力).<ref>[[Hiraizumi Kiyomi]] used the term "Shaji" and "Shaji Seiryoku" in his work *The Relationship Between Temples and Society in the Middle Ages* (1926).</ref> These powers were significant during Japan's {{ill|medieval period|ja|日本の中世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, sharing authority with the {{ill|Bakufu|ja|武家政権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|qq=|1=samurai government|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545907}} and the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court of Japan]]. Unlike centralized political entities, the religious powers were decentralized, with each temple or shrine acting independently. Even within a single temple or shrine, various factions often vied for dominance, which is why the term "seiryoku" (power) is used.
== Overview == During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], major temples such as [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] established their own military forces, including [[:en:Sōhei|Sohei]] (warrior monks), and utilized demonstrations such as carrying [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] in {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goso|qq=}} to assert their demands.
Temples also maintained large entourages of lay followers known as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}, enabling significant economic, academic, and artistic activities. The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
[[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (modern [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]), dominated eastern Kyoto, while [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).
Within the temple domains, the [[:en:Right of asylum|Right of asylum]] regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan-funyu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, leaders like [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building [[:en:Azuchi Castle|Azuchi Castle]] and [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 刀狩|1=Sword hunt|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1133763}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.

[[ja:寺社勢力]]
<!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--> [[Category:Azuchi–Momoyama period]] [[Category:History of Buddhism in Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11458053}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社勢力]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11458053]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Jisha Seiryoku''' (寺社勢力) refers to the religious and temple powers of Japan, also known as '''Shaji Seiryoku''' (社寺勢力).<ref>[[Hiraizumi Kiyomi]] used the term "Shaji" and "Shaji Seiryoku" in his work *The Relationship Between Temples and Society in the Middle Ages* (1926).</ref> These powers were significant during Japan's {{ill|medieval period|ja|日本の中世|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, sharing authority with the {{ill|Bakufu|ja|武家政権|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|1=samurai government|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545907}} and the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court of Japan]]. Unlike centralized political entities, the religious powers were decentralized, with each temple or shrine acting independently. Even within a single temple or shrine, various factions often vied for dominance, which is why the term "seiryoku" (power) is used.
== Overview == During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], major temples such as [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] established their own military forces, including [[:en:Sōhei|Sohei]] (warrior monks), and utilized demonstrations such as carrying [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] in {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goso|qq=}} to assert their demands.
Temples also maintained large entourages of lay followers known as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}, enabling significant economic, academic, and artistic activities. The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
[[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (modern [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]), dominated eastern Kyoto, while [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).
Within the temple domains, the [[:en:Right of asylum|Right of asylum]] regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan-funyu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, leaders like [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building [[:en:Azuchi Castle|Azuchi Castle]] and [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 刀狩|1=Sword hunt|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1133763}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.
== Major Religious Powers == * [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] * [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (now [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]) * [[:en:Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu-dera]] * Honganji-ha]]) ** [[:en:Ishiyama Hongan-ji|Ishiyama Hongan-ji]] ** [[:en:Yamashina Mido|Yamashina Hongan-ji]] ** {{ill|Oyama Gobo|ja|尾山御坊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Gobo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金沢城|1=Kanazawa Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q511412}} ** [[:en:Yoshizaki-gobō|Yoshizaki Gobo]] ** [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō Ikki]] * Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} * [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] * [[:en:Mii-dera|Onjō-ji]] * [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] * [[:en:Three Mountains of Dewa|Dewa Sanzan]] * {{ill|Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisen-ji|ja|平泉寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisen-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平泉寺白山神社}} (now [[:en:Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|Heisen-ji Hakusan Shrine]]) * [[:en:Mount Sekidō|Isurugi-yama]] Tenpyō-ji (now {{ill|Isurugi Hikojinja|ja|伊須流岐比古神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isurugi Hikojinja|qq=}}) * {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnomine|1=Mount Tōnomine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430808}} Myōraku-ji (now [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]]) * {{ill|Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyama-dera|ja|大山寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyama-dera|qq=}} * [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] * [[:en:Itsukushima Shrine|Itsukushima Shrine]] *
