The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
[[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (modern [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]), dominated eastern Kyoto, while [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).
Within the temple domains, the [[:en:Right of asylum|Right of asylum]] regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan-funyu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, leaders like [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building [[:en:Azuchi Castle|Azuchi Castle]] and [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 刀狩|1=Sword hunt|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1133763}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.
== Major Religious Powers == * [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] * [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (now [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]) * [[:en:Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu-dera]] * [[:en:Iwashimizu <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社勢力]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11458053]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Jisha Seiryoku''' (寺社勢力) refers to the religious and temple powers of Japan, also known as '''Shaji Seiryoku''' (社寺勢力).<ref>[[Hiraizumi Kiyomi]] used the term "Shaji" and "Shaji Seiryoku" in his work *The Relationship Between Temples and Society in the Middle Ages* (1926).</ref> These powers were significant during Japan's {{ill|medieval period|ja|日本の中世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, sharing authority with the {{ill|Bakufu|ja|武家政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|1=samurai government|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545907}} and the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court of Japan]]. Unlike centralized political entities, the religious powers were decentralized, with each temple or shrine acting independently. Even within a single temple or shrine, various factions often vied for dominance, which is why the term "seiryoku" (power) is used.
== Overview == During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], major temples such as [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] established their own military forces, including [[:en:Sōhei|Sohei]] (warrior monks), and utilized demonstrations such as carrying [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] in {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goso|qq=}} to assert their demands.
Temples also maintained large entourages of lay followers known as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}, enabling significant economic, academic, and artistic activities. The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
[[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (modern [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]), dominated eastern Kyoto, while [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).
Within the temple domains, the [[:en:Right of asylum|Right of asylum]] regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan-funyu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, leaders like [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building [[:en:Azuchi Castle|Azuchi Castle]] and [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 刀狩|1=Sword hunt|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1133763}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.
== Major Religious Powers == * [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] * [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (now [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]) * [[:en:Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu-dera]] * [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[:en:Shitennō-ji|Shitennō-ji]] * [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] * [[:en:Ikkō-shū|Ikkō-shū]] ([[:en:Jodo Shinshu Hongwanji-ha|Jōdo Shinshū Honganji-ha]]) ** [[:en:Ishiyama Hongan-ji|Ishiyama Hongan-ji]] ** [[:en:Yamashina Mido|Yamashina Hongan-ji]] ** {{ill|Oyama Gobo|ja|尾山御坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Gobo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金沢城|1=Kanazawa Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q511412}} ** [[:en:Yoshizaki-gobō|Yoshizaki Gobo]] ** [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō Ikki]] * [[:en:Senju-ji|Senchū-ji]] ({{ill|Shinshū Takada-ha|ja|真宗高田派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshū Takada-ha|qq=}}) * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kimpusen-ji]] (Yoshino) * [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] * [[:en:Daigo-ji|Daigo-ji]] * [[:en:Kongōbu-ji|Kongōbu-ji]] ([[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]) * {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} * [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] * [[:en:Mii-dera|Onjō-ji]] * [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] * [[:en:Three Mountains of Dewa|Dewa Sanzan]] * {{ill|Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisenji Hakusan Tōnomine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430808}} Myōraku-ji (now [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]]) * {{ill|Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyama-dera|ja|大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyama-dera|qq=}} * [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] * [[:en:Itsukushima Shrine|Itsukushima Shrine]] * [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingū]] * [[:en:Aso Shrine|Aso Shrine]] * [[:en:Mount Hiko|Hiko-san]] Reisō-ji

== Bibliography == * [[:en:Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|Kuroda Toshio]]. *Temple and Shrine Powers: Another Medieval Society.* Iwanami Shinsho, 1980. * {{ill|Itō Masatoshi|ja|伊藤正敏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Masatoshi|qq=}}. *The Medieval Religious Powers: Migration and Connection.* Chikuma Shinsho, 2008. == See Also == * {{ill|Kenmitsu System|ja|顕密体制|ja|顕密体制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmitsu System|qq=}} * {{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制|ja|権門体制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon System Theory|qq=}} * [[:en:Right of asylum|Right of asylum]] * {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|ja|境内都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple Precinct Cities|qq=}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|fr|Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|lt=Teramachi|ja|寺内町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaimachi|qq=}} * [[:en:Sōhei|Sōhei]] * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}

<!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--> [[Category:Azuchi–Momoyama period]] [[Category:History of Buddhism in Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11458053}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社勢力]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11458053]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Jisha Seiryoku''' (寺社勢力) refers to the religious and temple powers of Japan, also known as '''Shaji Seiryoku''' (社寺勢力).<ref>[[Hiraizumi Kiyomi]] used the term "Shaji" and "Shaji Seiryoku" in his work *The Relationship Between Temples and Society in the Middle Ages* (1926).</ref> These powers were significant during Japan's {{ill|medieval period|ja|日本の中世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, sharing authority with the {{ill|Bakufu|ja|武家政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|1=samurai government|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545907}} and the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court of Japan]]. Unlike centralized political entities, the religious powers were decentralized, with each temple or shrine acting independently. Even within a single temple or shrine, various factions often vied for dominance, which is why the term "seiryoku" (power) is used.
== Overview == During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], major temples such as [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] established their own military forces, including [[:en:Sōhei|Sohei]] (warrior monks), and utilized demonstrations such as carrying [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] in {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goso|qq=}} to assert their demands.
Temples also maintained large entourages of lay followers known as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}, enabling significant economic, academic, and artistic activities. The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
[[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (modern [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]), dominated eastern Kyoto, while [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).
Within the temple domains, the [[:en:Right of asylum|Right of asylum]] regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan-funyu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, leaders like [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building [[:en:Azuchi Castle|Azuchi Castle]] and [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 刀狩|1=Sword hunt|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1133763}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.

Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 刀狩|1=Sword hunt|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1133763}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.
== Major Religious Powers == * [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] * [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka <!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--> [[Category:Azuchi–Momoyama period]] [[Category:History of Buddhism in Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11458053}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社勢力]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11458053]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Jisha Seiryoku''' (寺社勢力) refers to the religious and temple powers of Japan, also known as '''Shaji Seiryoku''' (社寺勢力).<ref>[[Hiraizumi Kiyomi]] used the term "Shaji" and "Shaji Seiryoku" in his work *The Relationship Between Temples and Society in the Middle Ages* (1926).</ref> These powers were significant during Japan's {{ill|medieval period|ja|日本の中世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, sharing authority with the {{ill|Bakufu|ja|武家政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|1=samurai government|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545907}} and the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court of Japan]]. Unlike centralized political entities, the religious powers were decentralized, with each temple or shrine acting independently. Even within a single temple or shrine, various factions often vied for dominance, which is why the term "seiryoku" (power) is used.
== Overview == During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], major temples such as [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] established their own military forces, including [[:en:Sōhei|Sohei]] (warrior monks), and utilized demonstrations such as carrying [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] in {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goso|qq=}} to assert their demands.
Temples also maintained large entourages of lay followers known as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}, enabling significant economic, academic, and artistic activities. The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
[[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (modern [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]), dominated eastern Kyoto, while [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).
Within the temple domains, the [[:en:Right of asylum|Right of asylum]] regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan-funyu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, leaders like [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building [[:en:Azuchi Castle|Azuchi Castle]] and [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 刀狩|1=Sword hunt|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1133763}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.
== Major Religious Powers == * [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] * [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (now [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]) * [[:en:Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu-dera]] * [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[:en:Shitennō-ji|Shitennō-ji]] * [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] * [[:en:Ikkō-shū|Ikkō-shū]] ([[:en:Jodo Shinshu Hongwanji-ha|Jōdo Shinshū Honganji-ha]]) ** [[:en:Ishiyama Hongan-ji|Ishiyama Hongan-ji]] ** [[:en:Yamashina Mido|Yamashina Hongan-ji]] ** {{ill|Oyama Gobo|ja|尾山御坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Gobo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金沢城|1=Kanazawa Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q511412}} ** [[:en:Yoshizaki-gobō|Yoshizaki Gobo]] ** [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō Ikki]] * [[:en:Senju-ji|Senchū-ji]] ({{ill|Shinshū Takada-ha|ja|真宗高田派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshū Takada-ha|qq=}}) * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kimpusen-ji]] (Yoshino) * [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] * [[:en:Daigo-ji|Daigo-ji]] * [[:en:Kongōbu-ji|Kongōbu-ji]] ([[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]) * {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} * [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] * [[:en:Mii-dera|Onjō-ji]] * [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] * [[:en:Three Mountains of Dewa|Dewa Sanzan]] * {{ill|Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisen-ji|ja|平泉寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisen-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平泉寺白山神社}} (now [[:en:Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|Heisen-ji Hakusan Shrine]]) * [[:en:Mount Sekidō|Isurugi-yama]] Tenpyō-ji (now {{ill|Isurugi Hikojinja|ja|伊須流岐比古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isurugi Hikojinja|qq=}}) * {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnomine|1=Mount Tōnomine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430808}} Myōraku-ji (now [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]]) * {{ill|Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyama-dera|ja|大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyama-dera|qq=}} * [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] * [[:en:Itsukushima Shrine|Itsukushima Shrine]] * [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingū]] * [[:en:Aso Shrine|Aso Shrine]] * [[:en:Mount Hiko|Hiko-san]] Reisō-ji

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社勢力]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11458053]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Jisha Seiryoku''' (寺社勢力) refers to the religious and temple powers of Japan, also known as '''Shaji Seiryoku''' (社寺勢力).<ref>[[Hiraizumi Kiyomi]] used the term "Shaji" and "Shaji Seiryoku" in his work *The Relationship Between Temples and Society in the Middle Ages* (1926).</ref> These powers were significant during Japan's {{ill|medieval period|ja|日本の中世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, sharing authority with the {{ill|Bakufu|ja|武家政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|1=samurai government|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545907}} and the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court of Japan]]. Unlike centralized political entities, the religious powers were decentralized, with each temple or shrine acting independently. Even within a single temple or shrine, various factions often vied for dominance, which is why the term "seiryoku" (power) is used.
== Overview == During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], major temples such as [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] established their own military forces, including [[:en:Sōhei|Sohei]] (warrior monks), and utilized demonstrations such as carrying [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] in {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goso|qq=}} to assert their demands.
Temples also maintained large entourages of lay followers known as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}, enabling significant economic, academic, and artistic activities. The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
[[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (modern [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]), dominated eastern Kyoto, while [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).
Within the temple domains, the [[:en:Right of asylum|Right of asylum]] regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan-funyu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, leaders like [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building [[:en:Azuchi Castle|Azuchi Castle]] and [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 刀狩|1=Sword hunt|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1133763}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.
== Major Religious Powers == * [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] * [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (now [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]) * [[:en:Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu-dera]] * [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[:en:Shitennō-ji|Shitennō-ji]] * [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] * [[:en:Ikkō-shū|Ikkō-shū]] ([[:en:Jodo Shinshu Hongwanji-ha|Jōdo Shinshū Honganji-ha]]) ** [[:en:Ishiyama Hongan-ji|Ishiyama Hongan-ji]] ** [[:en:Yamashina Mido|Yamashina Hongan-ji]] ** {{ill|Oyama Gobo|ja|尾山御坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Gobo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金沢城|1=Kanazawa Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q511412}} ** [[:en:Yoshizaki-gobō|Yoshizaki Gobo]] ** [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō Ikki]] * [[:en:Senju-ji|Senchū-ji]] ({{ill|Shinshū Takada-ha|ja|真宗高田派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshū Takada-ha|qq=}}) * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kimpusen-ji]] (Yoshino) * [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] * [[:en:Daigo-ji|Daigo-ji]] * [[:en:Kongōbu-ji|Kongōbu-ji]] ([[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]) * {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} * [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] * [[:en:Mii-dera|Onjō-ji]] * [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] * [[:en:Three Mountains of Dewa|Dewa Sanzan]] * {{ill|Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisen-ji|ja|平泉寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisen-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平泉寺白山神社}} (now [[:en:Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|Heisen-ji Hakusan Shrine]]) * [[:en:Mount Sekidō|Isurugi-yama]] Tenpyō-ji (now {{ill|Isurugi Hikojinja|ja|伊須流岐比古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isurugi Hikojinja|qq=}}) * {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnomine|1=Mount Tōnomine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430808}} Myōraku-ji (now [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]]) * {{ill|Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyama-dera|ja|大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyama-dera|qq=}} * [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] * [[:en:Itsukushima Shrine|Itsukushima Shrine]] * [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingū]] * [[:en:Aso Shrine|Aso Shrine]] * [[:en:Mount Hiko|Hiko-san]] Reisō-ji

Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan-funyu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, leaders like [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building [[:en:Azuchi Castle|Azuchi Castle]] and [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 刀狩|1=Sword hunt|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1133763}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.
== Major Religious Powers == * [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] * [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (now [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]) * [[:en:Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu-dera]] * [[:en:Iwashimizu <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社勢力]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11458053]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Jisha Seiryoku''' (寺社勢力) refers to the religious and temple powers of Japan, also known as '''Shaji Seiryoku''' (社寺勢力).<ref>[[Hiraizumi Kiyomi]] used the term "Shaji" and "Shaji Seiryoku" in his work *The Relationship Between Temples and Society in the Middle Ages* (1926).</ref> These powers were significant during Japan's {{ill|medieval period|ja|日本の中世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, sharing authority with the {{ill|Bakufu|ja|武家政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|1=samurai government|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545907}} and the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court of Japan]]. Unlike centralized political entities, the religious powers were decentralized, with each temple or shrine acting independently. Even within a single temple or shrine, various factions often vied for dominance, which is why the term "seiryoku" (power) is used.
== Overview == During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], major temples such as [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] established their own military forces, including [[:en:Sōhei|Sohei]] (warrior monks), and utilized demonstrations such as carrying [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] in {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goso|qq=}} to assert their demands.
Temples also maintained large entourages of lay followers known as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}, enabling significant economic, academic, and artistic activities. The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
[[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (modern [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]), dominated eastern Kyoto, while [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).
Within the temple domains, the [[:en:Right of asylum|Right of asylum]] regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan-funyu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, leaders like [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building [[:en:Azuchi Castle|Azuchi Castle]] and [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 刀狩|1=Sword hunt|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1133763}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.
== Major Religious Powers == * [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] * [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (now [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]) * [[:en:Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu-dera]] * [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[:en:Shitennō-ji|Shitennō-ji]] * [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] * [[:en:Ikkō-shū|Ikkō-shū]] ([[:en:Jodo Shinshu Hongwanji-ha|Jōdo Shinshū Honganji-ha]]) ** [[:en:Ishiyama Hongan-ji|Ishiyama Hongan-ji]] ** [[:en:Yamashina Mido|Yamashina Hongan-ji]] ** {{ill|Oyama Gobo|ja|尾山御坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Gobo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金沢城|1=Kanazawa Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q511412}} ** [[:en:Yoshizaki-gobō|Yoshizaki Gobo]] ** [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō Ikki]] * [[:en:Senju-ji|Senchū-ji]] ({{ill|Shinshū Takada-ha|ja|真宗高田派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshū Takada-ha|qq=}}) * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kimpusen-ji]] (Yoshino) * [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] * [[:en:Daigo-ji|Daigo-ji]] * [[:en:Kongōbu-ji|Kongōbu-ji]] ([[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]) * {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} * [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] * [[:en:Mii-dera|Onjō-ji]] * [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] * [[:en:Three Mountains of Dewa|Dewa Sanzan]] * {{ill|Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisen-ji|ja|平泉寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisen-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平泉寺白山神社}} (now [[:en:Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|Heisen-ji Hakusan Shrine]]) * [[:en:Mount Sekidō|Isurugi-yama]] Tenpyō-ji (now {{ill|Isurugi Hikojinja|ja|伊須流岐比古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isurugi Hikojinja|qq=}}) * {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnomine|1=Mount Tōnomine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430808}} Myōraku-ji (now [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]]) * {{ill|Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyama-dera|ja|大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyama-dera|qq=}} * [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] * [[:en:Itsukushima Shrine|Itsukushima Shrine]] * [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingū]] * [[:en:Aso Shrine|Aso Shrine]] * [[:en:Mount Hiko|Hiko-san]] Reisō-ji
== References == <references />
== Bibliography == * [[:en:Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|Kuroda Toshio]]. *Temple and Shrine Powers: Another Medieval Society.* Iwanami Shinsho, 1980. * {{ill|Itō Masatoshi|ja|伊藤正敏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Masatoshi|qq=}}. *The Medieval Religious Powers: Migration and Connection.* Chikuma Shinsho, 2008. == See Also == * {{ill|Kenmitsu System|ja|顕密体制|ja|顕密体制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmitsu System|qq=}} * {{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制|ja|権門体制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon System Theory|qq=}} * [[:en:Right of asylum|Right of asylum]] * {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|ja|境内都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple Precinct Cities|qq=}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|fr|Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|lt=Teramachi|ja|寺内町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaimachi|qq=}} * [[:en:Sōhei|Sōhei]] * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}

[[ja:寺社勢力]]
<!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--><!--Temple and Shrine Powers--> [[Category:Azuchi–Momoyama period]] [[Category:History of Buddhism in Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11458053}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社勢力]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社勢力]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社勢力]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11458053]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Jisha Seiryoku''' (寺社勢力) refers to the religious and temple powers of Japan, also known as '''Shaji Seiryoku''' (社寺勢力).<ref>[[Hiraizumi Kiyomi]] used the term "Shaji" and "Shaji Seiryoku" in his work *The Relationship Between Temples and Society in the Middle Ages* (1926).</ref> These powers were significant during Japan's {{ill|medieval period|ja|日本の中世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, sharing authority with the {{ill|Bakufu|ja|武家政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|1=samurai government|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545907}} and the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court of Japan]]. Unlike centralized political entities, the religious powers were decentralized, with each temple or shrine acting independently. Even within a single temple or shrine, various factions often vied for dominance, which is why the term "seiryoku" (power) is used.
== Overview == During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], major temples such as [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] established their own military forces, including [[:en:Sōhei|Sohei]] (warrior monks), and utilized demonstrations such as carrying [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] in {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goso|qq=}} to assert their demands.
Temples also maintained large entourages of lay followers known as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}, enabling significant economic, academic, and artistic activities. The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
[[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (modern [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]), dominated eastern Kyoto, while [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).
Within the temple domains, the [[:en:Right of asylum|Right of asylum]] regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan-funyu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, leaders like [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building [[:en:Azuchi Castle|Azuchi Castle]] and [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 刀狩|1=Sword hunt|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1133763}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.
== Major Religious Powers == * [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] * [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (now [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]) * [[:en:Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu-dera]] * [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[:en:Shitennō-ji|Shitennō-ji]] * [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] * [[:en:Ikkō-shū|Ikkō-shū]] ([[:en:Jodo Shinshu Hongwanji-ha|Jōdo Shinshū Honganji-ha]]) ** [[:en:Ishiyama Hongan-ji|Ishiyama Hongan-ji]] ** [[:en:Yamashina Mido|Yamashina Hongan-ji]] ** {{ill|Oyama Gobo|ja|尾山御坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Gobo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金沢城|1=Kanazawa Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q511412}} ** [[:en:Yoshizaki-gobō|Yoshizaki Gobo]] ** [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō Ikki]] * [[:en:Senju-ji|Senchū-ji]] ({{ill|Shinshū Takada-ha|ja|真宗高田派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshū Takada-ha|qq=}}) * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kimpusen-ji]] (Yoshino) * [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] * [[:en:Daigo-ji|Daigo-ji]] * * [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] * [[:en:Mii-dera|Onjō-ji]] * [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] * [[:en:Three Mountains of Dewa|Dewa Sanzan]] * {{ill|Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisen-ji|ja|平泉寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisen-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平泉寺白山神社}} (now [[:en:Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|Heisen-ji Hakusan Shrine]]) * [[:en:Mount Tōnomine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430808}} Myōraku-ji (now [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]]) * {{ill|Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyama-dera|ja|大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyama-dera|qq=}} * [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] * [[:en:Itsukushima Shrine|Itsukushima Shrine]] * [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingū]] * [[:en:Aso Shrine|Aso Shrine]] * [[:en:Mount Hiko|Hiko-san]] Reisō-ji

Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 刀狩|1=Sword hunt|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1133763}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.

{{wikidata link|Q11458053}} [[Category:Azuchi-Momoyama period]] [[Category:Azuchi–Momoyama period]] [[Category:Buddhism in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:History of Buddhism in Japan]] [[Category:Holy cities]] [[Category:Kamakura period]] [[Category:Muromachi period]] [[Category:Nanboku-chō period]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社勢力]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社勢力]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社勢力]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11458053]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Jisha Seiryoku''' (寺社勢力) refers to the religious and temple powers of Japan, also known as '''Shaji Seiryoku''' (社寺勢力).<ref>[[Hiraizumi Kiyomi]] used the term "Shaji" and "Shaji Seiryoku" in his work *The Relationship Between Temples and Society in the Middle Ages* (1926).</ref> These powers were significant during Japan's {{ill|medieval period|ja|日本の中世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, sharing authority with the {{ill|Bakufu|ja|武家政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|1=samurai government|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545907}} and the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court of Japan]]. Unlike centralized political entities, the religious powers were decentralized, with each temple or shrine acting independently. Even within a single temple or shrine, various factions often vied for dominance, which is why the term "seiryoku" (power) is used.
== Overview == During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], major temples such as [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] established their own military forces, including [[:en:Sōhei|Sohei]] (warrior monks), and utilized demonstrations such as carrying [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] in {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goso|qq=}} to assert their demands.
Temples also maintained large entourages of lay followers known as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}, enabling significant economic, academic, and artistic activities. The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
[[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (modern [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]), dominated eastern Kyoto, while [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).
Within the temple domains, the [[:en:Right of asylum|Right of asylum]] regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan-funyu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, leaders like [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building [[:en:Azuchi Castle|Azuchi Castle]] and [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 刀狩|1=Sword hunt|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1133763}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.

Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (now [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]) * [[:en:Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu-dera]] * [[:en:Iwashimizu {{wikidata link|Q11458053}} [[Category:Azuchi-Momoyama period]] [[Category:Azuchi–Momoyama period]] [[Category:Buddhism in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:History of Buddhism in Japan]] [[Category:Holy cities]] [[Category:Kamakura period]] [[Category:Muromachi period]] [[Category:Nanboku-chō period]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社勢力]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社勢力]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社勢力]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11458053]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Jisha Seiryoku''' (寺社勢力) refers to the religious and temple powers of Japan, also known as '''Shaji Seiryoku''' (社寺勢力).<ref>[[Hiraizumi Kiyomi]] used the term "Shaji" and "Shaji Seiryoku" in his work *The Relationship Between Temples and Society in the Middle Ages* (1926).</ref> These powers were significant during Japan's {{ill|medieval period|ja|日本の中世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, sharing authority with the {{ill|Bakufu|ja|武家政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|1=samurai government|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545907}} and the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court of Japan]]. Unlike centralized political entities, the religious powers were decentralized, with each temple or shrine acting independently. Even within a single temple or shrine, various factions often vied for dominance, which is why the term "seiryoku" (power) is used.
== Overview == During the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], major temples such as [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] established their own military forces, including [[:en:Sōhei|Sohei]] (warrior monks), and utilized demonstrations such as carrying [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] in {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goso|qq=}} to assert their demands.
Temples also maintained large entourages of lay followers known as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}, enabling significant economic, academic, and artistic activities. The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
[[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (modern [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]), dominated eastern Kyoto, while [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).
Within the temple domains, the [[:en:Right of asylum|Right of asylum]] regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan-funyu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, leaders like [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building [[:en:Azuchi Castle|Azuchi Castle]] and [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] firearms for military campaigns.
Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 刀狩|1=Sword hunt|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1133763}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.
== Major Religious Powers == * [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]] * [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (now [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]) * [[:en:Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu-dera]] * [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[:en:Shitennō-ji|Shitennō-ji]] * [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] * [[:en:Ikkō-shū|Ikkō-shū]] ([[:en:Jodo Shinshu Hongwanji-ha|Jōdo Shinshū Honganji-ha]]) ** [[:en:Ishiyama Hongan-ji|Ishiyama Hongan-ji]] ** [[:en:Yamashina Mido|Yamashina Hongan-ji]] ** {{ill|Oyama Gobo|ja|尾山御坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Gobo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金沢城|1=Kanazawa Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q511412}} ** [[:en:Yoshizaki-gobō|Yoshizaki Gobo]] ** [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō Ikki]] * [[:en:Senju-ji|Senchū-ji]] ({{ill|Shinshū Takada-ha|ja|真宗高田派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshū Takada-ha|qq=}}) * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kimpusen-ji]] (Yoshino) * [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] * [[:en:Daigo-ji|Daigo-ji]] * [[:en:Kongōbu-ji|Kongōbu-ji]] ([[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]) * {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} * [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] * [[:en:Mii-dera|Onjō-ji]] * [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] * [[:en:Three Mountains of Dewa|Dewa Sanzan]] * {{ill|Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisen-ji|ja|平泉寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisen-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平泉寺白山神社}} (now [[:en:Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|Heisen-ji Hakusan Shrine]]) * [[:en:Mount Sekidō|Isurugi-yama]] Tenpyō-ji (now {{ill|Isurugi Hikojinja|ja|伊須流岐比古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isurugi Hikojinja|qq=}}) * {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnomine|1=Mount Tōnomine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430808}} Myōraku-ji (now [[:en:Tanzan Shrine|Tanzan Shrine]]) * {{ill|Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyama-dera|ja|大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyama-dera|qq=}} * [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] * [[:en:Itsukushima Shrine|Itsukushima Shrine]] * [[:en:Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]] * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingū]] * [[:en:Aso Shrine|Aso Shrine]] * [[:en:Mount Hiko|Hiko-san]] Reisō-ji
== References == <references />
== Bibliography == * [[:en:Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|Kuroda Toshio]]. *Temple and Shrine Powers: Another Medieval Society.* Iwanami Shinsho, 1980. * {{ill|Itō Masatoshi|ja|伊藤正敏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Masatoshi|qq=}}. *The Medieval Religious Powers: Migration and Connection.* Chikuma Shinsho, 2008. == See Also == * {{ill|Kenmitsu System|ja|顕密体制|ja|顕密体制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmitsu System|qq=}} * {{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制|ja|権門体制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon System Theory|qq=}} * [[:en:Right of asylum|Right of asylum]] * {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|ja|境内都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple Precinct Cities|qq=}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|fr|Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|lt=Teramachi|ja|寺内町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaimachi|qq=}} * [[:en:Sōhei|Sōhei]] * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社勢力]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社勢力]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社勢力]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11458053]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Jisha Seiryoku''' (寺社勢力) refers to the religious and temple powers of Japan, also known as '''Shaji Seiryoku''' (社寺勢力).<ref>[[Hiraizumi Kiyomi]] used the term "Shaji" and "Shaji Seiryoku" in his work *The Relationship Between Temples and Society in the Middle Ages* (1926).</ref> These powers were significant during Japan's {{ill|medieval period|ja|日本の中世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, sharing authority with the {{ill|Bakufu|ja|武家政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|1=samurai government|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545907}} and the {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|WD=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court of Japan}}. Unlike centralized political entities, the religious powers were decentralized, with each temple or shrine acting independently. Even within a single temple or shrine, various factions often vied for dominance, which is why the term "seiryoku" (power) is used.

== Overview ==
During the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, major temples such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji}} and {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji}} established their own military forces, including {{ill|Sōhei|en|Sōhei|WD=Q1475695|lt=Sohei}} (warrior monks), and utilized demonstrations such as carrying {{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi}} in {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goso|qq=}} to assert their demands.

Temples also maintained large entourages of lay followers known as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}, enabling significant economic, academic, and artistic activities. The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.

{{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji}} and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (modern {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|WD=Q692714|lt=Yasaka Shrine}}), dominated eastern Kyoto, while {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji}} controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).

Within the temple domains, the {{ill|Right of asylum|en|Right of asylum|WD=Q11283415|lt=Right of asylum}} regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|en|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|WD=Q310445|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitsune}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}}, as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan-funyu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, leaders like {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building {{ill|Azuchi Castle|en|Azuchi Castle|WD=Q786888|lt=Azuchi Castle}} and {{ill|Negoro-ji|en|Negoro-ji|WD=Q1552446|lt=Negoro-ji}} firearms for military campaigns.

Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 刀狩|1=Sword hunt|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1133763}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.

== Major Religious Powers ==
* {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|WD=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei}} {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji}}
* {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha}}
* {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (now {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|WD=Q692714|lt=Yasaka Shrine}})
* {{ill|Kiyomizu-dera|en|Kiyomizu-dera|WD=Q221716|lt=Kiyomizu-dera}}
* {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|WD=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachimangū}}
* {{ill|Shitennō-ji|en|Shitennō-ji|WD=Q339859|lt=Shitennō-ji}}
* {{ill|Tōdai-ji|en|Tōdai-ji|WD=Q460367|lt=Tōdai-ji}}
* {{ill|Ikkō-shū|en|Ikkō-shū|WD=Q5366010|lt=Ikkō-shū}} ({{ill|Jodo Shinshu Hongwanji-ha|en|Jodo Shinshu Hongwanji-ha|WD=Q10389102|lt=Jōdo Shinshū Honganji-ha}})
** {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji|en|Ishiyama Hongan-ji|WD=Q1098123|lt=Ishiyama Hongan-ji}}
** {{ill|Yamashina Mido|en|Yamashina Mido|WD=Q7348557|lt=Yamashina Hongan-ji}}
** {{ill|Oyama Gobo|ja|尾山御坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Gobo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金沢城|1=Kanazawa Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q511412}}
** {{ill|Yoshizaki-gobō|en|Yoshizaki-gobō|WD=Q3572702|lt=Yoshizaki Gobo}}
** {{ill|Ikkō-ikki|en|Ikkō-ikki|WD=Q947533|lt=Ikkō Ikki}}
* {{ill|Senju-ji|en|Senju-ji|WD=Q3478562|lt=Senchū-ji}} ({{ill|Shinshū Takada-ha|ja|真宗高田派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshū Takada-ha|qq=}})
* {{ill|Kimpusen-ji|en|Kimpusen-ji|WD=Q1741668|lt=Kimpusen-ji}} (Yoshino)
* {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji}}
* {{ill|Daigo-ji|en|Daigo-ji|WD=Q1157535|lt=Daigo-ji}}
* {{ill|Kongōbu-ji|en|Kongōbu-ji|WD=Q1203429|lt=Kongōbu-ji}} ({{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya}})
* {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}
* {{ill|Shōgo-in|en|Shōgo-in|WD=Q8664338|lt=Shōgo-in Temple}}
* {{ill|Mii-dera|en|Mii-dera|WD=Q1756307|lt=Onjō-ji}}
* {{ill|Negoro-ji|en|Negoro-ji|WD=Q1552446|lt=Negoro-ji}}
* {{ill|Three Mountains of Dewa|en|Three Mountains of Dewa|WD=Q1155726|lt=Dewa Sanzan}}
* {{ill|Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisen-ji|ja|平泉寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisen-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平泉寺白山神社}} (now {{ill|Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|en|Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|WD=Q11483206|lt=Heisen-ji Hakusan Shrine}})
* {{ill|Mount Sekidō|en|Mount Sekidō|WD=Q11585200|lt=Isurugi-yama}} Tenpyō-ji (now {{ill|Isurugi Hikojinja|ja|伊須流岐比古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isurugi Hikojinja|qq=}})
* {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnomine|1=Mount Tōnomine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430808}} Myōraku-ji (now {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|en|Tanzan Shrine|WD=Q488618|lt=Tanzan Shrine}})
* {{ill|Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyama-dera|ja|大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyama-dera|qq=}}
* {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha}}
* {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|en|Itsukushima Shrine|WD=Q191763|lt=Itsukushima Shrine}}
* {{ill|Munakata Taisha|en|Munakata Taisha|WD=Q704962|lt=Munakata Taisha}}
* {{ill|Usa Jingū|en|Usa Jingū|WD=Q715632|lt=Usa Jingū}}
* {{ill|Aso Shrine|en|Aso Shrine|WD=Q715639|lt=Aso Shrine}}
* {{ill|Mount Hiko|en|Mount Hiko|WD=Q3077886|lt=Hiko-san}} Reisō-ji

Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Jisha Seiryoku''' (寺社勢力) refers to the religious and temple powers of Japan, also known as '''Shaji Seiryoku''' (社寺勢力).<ref>[[Hiraizumi Kiyomi]] used the term "Shaji" and "Shaji Seiryoku" in his work *The Relationship Between Temples and Society in the Middle Ages* (1926).</ref> These powers were significant during Japan's {{ill|medieval period|ja|日本の中世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, sharing authority with the {{ill|Bakufu|ja|武家政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|1=samurai government|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545907}} and the {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|WD=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court of Japan}}. Unlike centralized political entities, the religious powers were decentralized, with each temple or shrine acting independently. Even within a single temple or shrine, various factions often vied for dominance, which is why the term "seiryoku" (power) is used.

== Overview ==
During the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, major temples such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji}} and {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji}} established their own military forces, including {{ill|Sōhei|en|Sōhei|WD=Q1475695|lt=Sohei}} (warrior monks), and utilized demonstrations such as carrying {{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi}} in {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goso|qq=}} to assert their demands.

Temples also maintained large entourages of lay followers known as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}, enabling significant economic, academic, and artistic activities. The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.

{{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji}} and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (modern {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|WD=Q692714|lt=Yasaka Shrine}}), dominated eastern Kyoto, while {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji}} controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).

Within the temple domains, the {{ill|Right of asylum|en|Right of asylum|WD=Q11283415|lt=Right of asylum}} regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|en|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|WD=Q310445|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitsune}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}}, as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan-funyu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, leaders like {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building {{ill|Azuchi Castle|en|Azuchi Castle|WD=Q786888|lt=Azuchi Castle}} and {{ill|Negoro-ji|en|Negoro-ji|WD=Q1552446|lt=Negoro-ji}} firearms for military campaigns.

Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 刀狩|1=Sword hunt|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1133763}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社勢力]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社勢力]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社勢力]]

'''Jisha Seiryoku''' (寺社勢力) refers to the religious and temple powers of Japan, also known as '''Shaji Seiryoku''' (社寺勢力).<ref>[[Hiraizumi Kiyomi]] used the term "Shaji" and "Shaji Seiryoku" in his work *The Relationship Between Temples and Society in the Middle Ages* (1926).</ref> These powers were significant during Japan's {{ill|medieval period|ja|日本の中世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, sharing authority with the {{ill|Bakufu|ja|武家政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|1=samurai government|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545907}} and the {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|WD=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court of Japan}}. Unlike centralized political entities, the religious powers were decentralized, with each temple or shrine acting independently. Even within a single temple or shrine, various factions often vied for dominance, which is why the term "seiryoku" (power) is used.

== Overview ==
During the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, major temples such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji}} and {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji}} established their own military forces, including {{ill|Sōhei|en|Sōhei|WD=Q1475695|lt=Sohei}} (warrior monks), and utilized demonstrations such as carrying {{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi}} in {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goso|qq=}} to assert their demands.

Temples also maintained large entourages of lay followers known as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}, enabling significant economic, academic, and artistic activities. The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.

{{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji}} and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (modern {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|WD=Q692714|lt=Yasaka Shrine}}), dominated eastern Kyoto, while {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji}} controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).

Within the temple domains, the {{ill|Right of asylum|en|Right of asylum|WD=Q11283415|lt=Right of asylum}} regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|en|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|WD=Q310445|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitsune}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}}, as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan-funyu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, leaders like {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building {{ill|Azuchi Castle|en|Azuchi Castle|WD=Q786888|lt=Azuchi Castle}} and {{ill|Negoro-ji|en|Negoro-ji|WD=Q1552446|lt=Negoro-ji}} firearms for military campaigns.

Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 刀狩|1=Sword hunt|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1133763}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.

== Major Religious Powers ==
* {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|WD=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei}} {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji}}
* {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha}}
* {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (now {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|WD=Q692714|lt=Yasaka Shrine}})
* {{ill|Kiyomizu-dera|en|Kiyomizu-dera|WD=Q221716|lt=Kiyomizu-dera}}
* {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|WD=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachimangū}}

* {{ill|Ikkō-shū|en|Ikkō-shū|WD=Q5366010|lt=Ikkō-shū}} ({{ill|Jodo Shinshu Hongwanji-ha|en|Jodo Shinshu Hongwanji-ha|WD=Q10389102|lt=Jōdo Shinshū Honganji-ha}})
** {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji|en|Ishiyama Hongan-ji|WD=Q1098123|lt=Ishiyama Hongan-ji}}
** {{ill|Yamashina Mido|en|Yamashina Mido|WD=Q7348557|lt=Yamashina Hongan-ji}}
** {{ill|Oyama Gobo|ja|尾山御坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Gobo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金沢城|1=Kanazawa Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q511412}}
** {{ill|Yoshizaki-gobō|en|Yoshizaki-gobō|WD=Q3572702|lt=Yoshizaki Gobo}}
** {{ill|Ikkō-ikki|en|Ikkō-ikki|WD=Q947533|lt=Ikkō Ikki}}
* {{ill|Senju-ji|en|Senju-ji|WD=Q3478562|lt=Senchū-ji}} ({{ill|Shinshū Takada-ha|ja|真宗高田派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshū Takada-ha|qq=}})
* {{ill|Kimpusen-ji|en|Kimpusen-ji|WD=Q1741668|lt=Kimpusen-ji}} (Yoshino)
* {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji}}
* {{ill|Daigo-ji|en|Daigo-ji|WD=Q1157535|lt=Daigo-ji}}
* {{ill|Kongōbu-ji|en|Kongōbu-ji|WD=Q1203429|lt=Kongōbu-ji}} ({{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya}})
* {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}
* {{ill|Shōgo-in|en|Shōgo-in|WD=Q8664338|lt=Shōgo-in Temple}}
* {{ill|Mii-dera|en|Mii-dera|WD=Q1756307|lt=Onjō-ji}}
* {{ill|Negoro-ji|en|Negoro-ji|WD=Q1552446|lt=Negoro-ji}}
* {{ill|Three Mountains of Dewa|en|Three Mountains of Dewa|WD=Q1155726|lt=Dewa Sanzan}}
* {{ill|Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisen-ji|ja|平泉寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisen-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平泉寺白山神社}} (now {{ill|Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|en|Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|WD=Q11483206|lt=Heisen-ji Hakusan Shrine}})
* {{ill|Mount Sekidō|en|Mount Sekidō|WD=Q11585200|lt=Isurugi-yama}} Tenpyō-ji (now {{ill|Isurugi Hikojinja|ja|伊須流岐比古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isurugi Hikojinja|qq=}})
* {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnomine|1=Mount Tōnomine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430808}} Myōraku-ji (now {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|en|Tanzan Shrine|WD=Q488618|lt=Tanzan Shrine}})
* {{ill|Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyama-dera|ja|大山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyama-dera|qq=}}
* {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha}}
* {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|en|Itsukushima Shrine|WD=Q191763|lt=Itsukushima Shrine}}
* {{ill|Munakata Taisha|en|Munakata Taisha|WD=Q704962|lt=Munakata Taisha}}
* {{ill|Usa Jingū|en|Usa Jingū|WD=Q715632|lt=Usa Jingū}}
* {{ill|Aso Shrine|en|Aso Shrine|WD=Q715639|lt=Aso Shrine}}
* {{ill|Mount Hiko|en|Mount Hiko|WD=Q3077886|lt=Hiko-san}} Reisō-ji

== Major Religious Powers ==

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社勢力]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社勢力]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社勢力]]

'''Jisha Seiryoku''' (寺社勢力) refers to the religious and temple powers of Japan, also known as '''Shaji Seiryoku''' (社寺勢力).<ref>[[Hiraizumi Kiyomi]] used the term "Shaji" and "Shaji Seiryoku" in his work *The Relationship Between Temples and Society in the Middle Ages* (1926).</ref> These powers were significant during Japan's {{ill|medieval period|ja|日本の中世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, sharing authority with the {{ill|Bakufu|ja|武家政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|1=samurai government|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545907}} and the {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|WD=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court of Japan|1=Royal court}}. Unlike centralized political entities, the religious powers were decentralized, with each temple or shrine acting independently. Even within a single temple or shrine, various factions often vied for dominance, which is why the term "seiryoku" (power) is used.

== Overview ==
During the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, major temples such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji|1=Enryaku-ji}} and {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji|1=Kōfuku-ji}} established their own military forces, including {{ill|Sōhei|en|Sōhei|WD=Q1475695|lt=Sohei|1=Sōhei}} (warrior monks), and utilized demonstrations such as carrying {{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi|1=Mikoshi}} in {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goso|qq=}} to assert their demands.

Temples also maintained large entourages of lay followers known as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinto|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}, enabling significant economic, academic, and artistic activities. The areas surrounding these temples, now recognized as {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.

{{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji|1=Enryaku-ji}} and its subordinate shrine, {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (modern {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|WD=Q692714|lt=Yasaka Shrine|1=Yasaka Shrine}}), dominated eastern Kyoto, while {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji|1=Kōfuku-ji}} controlled nearly all of Yamato Province’s estates. These two powerful entities were collectively known as the "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺).

Within the temple domains, the {{ill|Right of asylum|en|Right of asylum|WD=Q11283415|lt=Right of asylum|1=Right of asylum}} regions attracted impoverished civilians and marginalized individuals. Temples also provided refuge to fugitives, such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|en|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|WD=Q310445|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitsune|1=Minamoto no Yoshitsune}} and {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}}, as government authority was limited within these areas due to rights like {{ill|Kendan-funyu|ja|検断不入|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan-funyu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, leaders like {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} clashed with temple powers. Despite their opposition, they utilized temple resources, such as Enryaku-ji craftsmen for building {{ill|Azuchi Castle|en|Azuchi Castle|WD=Q786888|lt=Azuchi Castle|1=Azuchi Castle}} and {{ill|Negoro-ji|en|Negoro-ji|WD=Q1552446|lt=Negoro-ji|1=Negoro-ji}} firearms for military campaigns.

Hideyoshi's 1585–1588 {{ill|Sword Hunt|ja|刀狩令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sword Hunt|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 刀狩|1=Sword hunt|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1133763}} included the disarmament of temple forces, effectively ending the five-century-long dominance of Jisha Seiryoku within Japan's power structures.

== Major Religious Powers ==
* {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|WD=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei|1=Mount Hiei}} {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji|1=Enryaku-ji}}
* {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha|1=Hiyoshi Taisha}}
* {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|lt=Yasaka Shrine|lt=Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}} (now {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|WD=Q692714|lt=Yasaka Shrine|1=Yasaka Shrine}})
* {{ill|Kiyomizu-dera|en|Kiyomizu-dera|WD=Q221716|lt=Kiyomizu-dera|1=Kiyomizu-dera}}

** {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji|en|Ishiyama Hongan-ji|WD=Q1098123|lt=Ishiyama Hongan-ji|1=Ishiyama Hongan-ji}}

** {{ill|Oyama Gobo|ja|尾山御坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Gobo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金沢城|1=Kanazawa Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q511412}}
** {{ill|Yoshizaki-gobō|en|Yoshizaki-gobō|WD=Q3572702|lt=Yoshizaki Gobo|1=Yoshizaki-gobō}}
** {{ill|Ikkō-ikki|en|Ikkō-ikki|WD=Q947533|lt=Ikkō Ikki|1=Ikkō-ikki}}
* {{ill|Senju-ji|en|Senju-ji|WD=Q3478562|lt=Senchū-ji|1=Senju-ji}} ({{ill|Shinshū Takada-ha|ja|真宗高田派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshū Takada-ha|qq=}})
* {{ill|Kimpusen-ji|en|Kimpusen-ji|WD=Q1741668|lt=Kimpusen-ji|1=Kimpusen-ji}} (Yoshino)
* {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji|1=Kōfuku-ji}}
* {{ill|Daigo-ji|en|Daigo-ji|WD=Q1157535|lt=Daigo-ji|1=Daigo-ji}}
* {{ill|Kongōbu-ji|en|Kongōbu-ji|WD=Q1203429|lt=Kongōbu-ji|1=Kongōbu-ji}} ({{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya|1=Mount Kōya}})
* {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}
* {{ill|Shōgo-in|en|Shōgo-in|WD=Q8664338|lt=Shōgo-in Temple|1=Shōgo-in}}
* {{ill|Mii-dera|en|Mii-dera|WD=Q1756307|lt=Onjō-ji|1=Mii-dera}}
* {{ill|Negoro-ji|en|Negoro-ji|WD=Q1552446|lt=Negoro-ji|1=Negoro-ji}}
* {{ill|Three Mountains of Dewa|en|Three Mountains of Dewa|WD=Q1155726|lt=Dewa Sanzan|1=Three Mountains of Dewa}}
* {{ill|Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|lt=Heisen-ji|ja|平泉寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisen-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平泉寺白山神社}} (now {{ill|Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|en|Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|WD=Q11483206|lt=Heisen-ji Hakusan Shrine|1=Heisenji Hakusan Shrine}})
* {{ill|Mount Sekidō|en|Mount Sekidō|WD=Q11585200|lt=Isurugi-yama|1=Mount Sekidō}} Tenpyō-ji (now {{ill|Isurugi Hikojinja|ja|伊須流岐比古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isurugi Hikojinja|qq=}})
* {{ill|Tōnomine|ja|多武峰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnomine|1=Mount Tonomine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430808}} Myōraku-ji (now {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|en|Tanzan Shrine|WD=Q488618|lt=Tanzan Shrine|1=Tanzan Shrine}})
